items.q: How did the feudal system work in Norman England?
items.correct: The king granted land to tenants-in-chief in return for knight service; they in turn enfeoffed knights who held land in return for military service to their lord.
items.misconception: Feudalism meant peasants were slaves owned by the king.
items.random_wrong: Feudalism was a written constitution introduced by William in 1066.
items.why: Feudalism described a tenurial chain of obligations among free landholders, not slavery. Most peasants were villeins tied to manors — a related but separate system.

items.q: Why did public health reforms accelerate after 1848?
items.correct: The 1848 Public Health Act followed cholera epidemics and Chadwick's report; John Snow's 1854 Broad Street pump study reinforced the case for clean water by 1875.
items.misconception: Public health acts were passed because Victorians discovered germs in the 1840s.
items.random_wrong: The 1875 Act made the NHS free for all workers.
items.why: Germ theory came later (Pasteur, Koch). 1848 reforms were driven by miasma theory plus pragmatic statistics, not bacteriology — students compress the discovery sequence.

items.q: Why did William of Normandy claim the English throne in 1066?
items.correct: He claimed Edward the Confessor had promised him the throne in 1051 and that Harold Godwinson had sworn an oath to support that claim in 1064.
items.misconception: William invaded because he was the strongest king in Europe and wanted more land.
items.random_wrong: William was Edward the Confessor's eldest son.
items.why: Students treat the invasion as raw conquest, missing that William framed it as enforcing a legitimate hereditary and oath-based claim — legitimacy mattered hugely in 11th-century politics.

items.q: When did the medieval period in Britain begin?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1066. “1215” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who was at the top of the feudal system?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The king. “The Pope” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If the medieval period began in 1066 and ended in 1485, how many years did it last?
items.why: Exact answer: 419. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
